Tomato.symptoms: Dark water-soaked spots on leaves, white fungal growth on leaf undersides, dark brown lesions on stems and fruit.
Tomato.explanation: Late blight is a devastating fungal-like disease caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. It spreads rapidly in cool, wet weather, destroying foliage and fruit. Fungal spores can be carried by wind and splash-water.

medicine.chemical: Fungicide spray containing Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Copper Octanoate.
medicine.biological: Application of Bacillus subtilis (e.g., Serenade ASO) or Trichoderma harzianum to boost plant immunity.

Tomato.fertilizer_advice: Reduce nitrogen applications as excess nitrogen encourages lush, susceptible foliage. Apply potassium to strengthen cell walls and increase resistance.
Tomato.irrigation_advice: Avoid overhead sprinkler irrigation which keeps foliage wet. Implement drip irrigation or water at the base early in the morning to allow leaves to dry quickly.

Tomato.symptoms: Pale green or yellow spots on the upper leaf surfaces, olive-green to brown velvety mold on the corresponding lower surfaces.
Tomato.explanation: Leaf mold, caused by the fungus Passalora fulva, primarily affects tomato plants grown in high humidity environments (like greenhouses). It causes premature leaf drop and weakens the plant.

medicine.chemical: Use Copper fungicides or Daconil (chlorothalonil) if the disease is severe.
medicine.biological: Spray neem oil solution or application of Pseudomonas fluorescens to foliage.

Tomato.fertilizer_advice: Maintain balanced N-P-K ratios. Ensure adequate calcium supply to prevent secondary infections.
Tomato.irrigation_advice: Prune lower leaves to improve air circulation and decrease humidity around the canopy. Keep watering consistent and avoid wetting leaves.

Potato.symptoms: Small, dark, circular spots with concentric rings (target-like pattern) on older leaves. Spots can merge, causing leaf yellowing and death.
Potato.explanation: Early blight is caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. It affects leaves, stems, and tubers, typically targeting stressed or older plants under alternating wet and dry conditions.

medicine.chemical: Fungicides containing Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, or Chlorothalonil.

Potato.fertilizer_advice: Maintain high soil fertility with adequate nitrogen and potassium. Stressed, nutrient-deficient potato plants are significantly more susceptible to Early Blight.
Potato.irrigation_advice: Avoid overhead watering. Water the crop during the day so foliage dries completely before nightfall.

Potato.symptoms: Water-soaked leaf lesions that turn black/brown, white downy growth on the underside of leaves under humid conditions, rotting tubers.
Potato.explanation: Caused by Phytophthora infestans (same pathogen as tomato late blight). This disease can wipe out entire fields within a few days under high moisture and temperature ranges of 15-20°C.

medicine.chemical: Apply systemic fungicides like Metalaxyl-M or Cymoxanil combined with protective Mancozeb.
medicine.biological: Copper hydroxide spray or Trichoderma-based soil treatments.

Potato.fertilizer_advice: Stop nitrogen fertilization immediately to curb vegetative growth. Apply soluble silica and potassium to toughen the potato vine tissue.
Potato.irrigation_advice: Do not irrigate during foggy or overcast periods. Monitor soil moisture closely and allow top soil to dry between waterings.

Wheat.symptoms: Small, oval, orange-brown pustules on the upper leaf surfaces, releasing powdery spores that easily rub off on fingers.
Wheat.explanation: Leaf rust, caused by the fungus Puccinia triticina, is the most common rust disease of wheat. It drains nutrients from the plant, reducing kernel size and grain yield.

medicine.chemical: Triazole-based fungicides (e.g., Tebuconazole, Propiconazole) or Strobilurins.
medicine.biological: Foliar application of Bacillus subtilis or garlic extract sprays (mild prevention).

Wheat.fertilizer_advice: Ensure balanced nutrition. Avoid excess nitrogen which delays maturity and makes the wheat crop highly susceptible to rust spores.
Wheat.irrigation_advice: Manage irrigation scheduling to prevent prolonged leaf wetness. Best to irrigate in the early morning hours.

Wheat.symptoms: White to light gray, powdery patches of fungal growth on leaves, stems, and heads. Older patches turn dull gray-brown.
Wheat.explanation: Caused by the fungus Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici. It thrives in cool, humid, shady conditions with dense crop stands.

medicine.chemical: Sulfur-based fungicides or systemic triazoles like Flutriafol.
medicine.biological: Bicarbonate sprays or bio-agents like Streptomyces.

Wheat.fertilizer_advice: Apply adequate potassium and silicon. Reduce seeding rate in future to prevent high canopy humidity.
Wheat.irrigation_advice: Ensure proper drainage and avoid waterlogging. Water when solar radiation is high to speed up evaporation.

Rice.symptoms: Spindle-shaped (diamond) lesions on leaves with reddish-brown borders and gray centers. Can also attack nodes and panicles (neck blast), causing them to break.
Rice.explanation: Rice blast is caused by the fungus Magnaporthe oryzae. It is one of the most destructive diseases of rice worldwide, favored by high relative humidity and warm temperatures.

medicine.chemical: Fungicide sprays containing Tricyclazole, Isoprothiolane, or Kasugamycin.
medicine.biological: Pseudomonas fluorescens formulation for seed treatment and foliar spray.

Rice.fertilizer_advice: Split nitrogen applications into 3-4 doses rather than a single large application. Maintain optimal soil potassium levels.
Rice.irrigation_advice: Keep fields flooded at a consistent level (avoid drought stress which triggers blast susceptibility). Drain and re-flood if neck rot begins to show.

Rice.symptoms: Oval, dark brown lesions on leaves, often with a yellow halo. Lesions are uniform and distributed across the leaf surface.
Rice.explanation: Brown spot is caused by the fungus Bipolaris oryzae. It is often referred to as a 'poor man's disease' because it typically occurs in soils that are nutrient-deficient, poorly drained, or dry.

medicine.chemical: Fungicides containing Mancozeb, Carbendazim, or Hexaconazole.
medicine.biological: Soil application of organic compost and PGPR (Plant Growth-Promoting Rhizobacteria).

Rice.fertilizer_advice: Correct nutritional deficiencies immediately. Apply nitrogen, potassium, and silicon to improve resistance.
Rice.irrigation_advice: Ensure adequate water supply. Prevent the soil from drying out completely. Improve field drainage if soil is waterlogged/anaerobic and toxic.

Cotton.symptoms: Upward or downward curling of leaf margins, thickening of leaf veins, and leaf-like growths (enations) on the underside of leaves.
Cotton.explanation: Cotton Leaf Curl Virus (CLCuV) is a destructive viral disease transmitted by the whitefly vector (Bemisia tabaci). It stunts cotton growth and severely reduces boll formation.

medicine.chemical: No chemical cure for the virus itself. Control the whitefly vector using insecticides like Imidacloprid, Acetamiprid, or Diafenthiuron.
medicine.biological: Yellow sticky traps for monitoring/trapping whiteflies. Spray neem-based formulation (Azadirachtin) or introduce natural predators like lacewings.

Cotton.fertilizer_advice: Apply micronutrient mixtures (specifically zinc and boron) to help the plant cope with viral stress. Increase potassium dosage.
Cotton.irrigation_advice: Maintain uniform soil moisture. Avoid water stress which attracts whiteflies. Keep fields weed-free as weeds act as alternative hosts for the virus.
